title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text: Schools could choose whether or not to use AI in October. The following analysis considers the results of pupils matched between the two sessions.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Descriptive Statistics and Score Distributions
The February results of the two groups were very similar. After using AI in October, the mean of the scores was again similar, but the spread narrowed for AI users.

title: APW Year 3 AI Report

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
section: Regression to the Mean and Progress Levels
text:
Regression to the Mean and Progress Levels
AI users had proportionally more pupils with a higher than average score in October making less than average progress. In other words, the regression to the mean effect is more pronounced for AI users.








Figure 3: Progress Levels from APW Feb to APW Oct by AI User Status









Table 3: Progress Levels from APW Feb to APW Oct by AI User Status

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Number of Comparisons & Infit Summary
The number of comparisons completed by AI was substantially higher for AI users than non-AI users. A higher number of comparisons is likely to lead to more reliable measurement. Infit greater than 1.3 is a measure of consistency of the judging. The percentage of pupils with infit over 1.3 was much lower for the group using AI. However, infit is sensitive to the number of comparisons made, so the difference does not necessarily reflect more consistency in the AI judging than the human.




Table 4: Comparisons Summary by AI User Status






mean_comparisons_feb_year_2
mean_comparisons_oct_year_3

pct_feb_year_2
pct_oct_year_3

title: APW Year 3 AI Report
text:
Conclusion
Overall, there is little to differentiate the performance of pupils whose schools used AI in October from those that did not. Both groups made similar average progress from February to October, with strong correlations between the two assessments.
The small differences that do appear, in the distributions and in the regression to the mean, may be explained by the higher number of comparisons completed by AI users, which is likely to lead to more reliable measurement.
What the analysis cannot show is whether the AI rewarded different skills or approaches to writing than human judges. Further qualitative analysis of the types of writing rewarded by each method would be needed to explore this question.

title: AI Enhanced CJ
text:
Conclusion


What we found

Similar average progress for both groups
Strong correlations in both groups (higher for AI users)
Regression to the mean more pronounced for AI users
More comparisons completed by AI → likely more reliable measurement


What remains open

Does AI reward different skills or writing approaches than human judges?
Further qualitative analysis needed to explore this question

APW 3 is the writing of pupils in Year 3, aged 7 to 8
Use this picture to write a story
Handwritten
In Feb of year 2 no AI was available
In Oct of year 3, schools could choose whether or not to use AI in October
The following analysis considers matched pupils between the two sessions


## Anchoring

We use an anchoring design where scripts are repeated between sessions
Scores are fixed, we calculate a shift constant between sessions to shift scores from the anchors
Same anchors for AI and non AI route

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Audio Feedback from Teachers summarised by AI: Individualised comments for every student.
The AI “Chloe” Reports: Direct feedback on multiple writing features (describing places, people, vocabulary) for every student.
Instructional Resources: Personalized multiple-choice questions and instructional guides on capitals, run-ons, and vocabulary.
Teacher Reports: Detailed analysis of the writing of every pupil.
The “Examiner” Summaries: AI-generated deep dives into general, strongest, and weakest responses — at class, school, and national level.

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
DeepMind Study: Method

5 UK secondary schools using the Eedi maths platform
Students chatted with a tutor after getting a question wrong



Condition 1
Human tutor

Condition 2
LLM tutor (human-supervised)

Condition 3
Static pre-written hint

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
DeepMind Study: Measurement
Effectiveness measured three ways:

Same question immediately after intervention
Follow-up chat → two attempts at a new question on the same topic
Success on the first question of the next unit

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
DeepMind Study: Results



Condition
Solved on 2nd attempt




Human tutor
91.2% [88.5%, 93.6%]


LearnLM (supervised)
93.0% [90.4%, 95.3%]


Static hint
65.4% [63.8%, 66.9%]




AI tutoring was as effective as human tutoring — both far outperformed static hints.

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Our Own Study

2,881 Year 6 pupils (aged 10–11)
Pre-test
Feedback
Post-test

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Pre-test

Students write a story in standardised conditions, without assistance
Assessed using Comparative Judgement by human teachers

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Feedback
Students receive an automated, personalised workbook with:

A paragraph of written feedback (teacher audio, processed by AI)
A checklist of writing features (AI-generated)
Multiple-choice questions on a writing aspect, with a short instructional resource

The exact MCQs depend on their standardised score

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Post-test Redraft

~1 month later, students rewrite their story
Reassessed using Comparative Judgement
Change in score is measured

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Overall Results
What percentage of pupils do you expect to improve their scores?
. . .

Correlation between assessments: 0.67
Mean score rose from 546 → 557 (+11 scaled score points)
Effect size: 0.34
62% of pupils improved their score

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Our AI Feedback Approach

Focus on questions not explanations
Trained on pedagogical approach
Targeted to ability
Personalise

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Focus on questions not explanations


Explanation
To make your place descriptions even better, try adding details about what Emma saw, heard, or felt in Ancient Greece. For example, you could tell us what the buildings looked like, if it was sunny or windy, or what sounds were around her.

Question
(Gap Fill) Emma stepped out of the time machine and looked around Ancient Greece. The air felt ______, and she could hear ______ in the distance.

warm and dusty; sandals tapping on stone streets

cold and snowy; waves crashing against ice

hot and humid; jungle birds calling

cool and foggy; car horns blaring

dry and smoky; fireworks popping overhead

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Trained on pedagogical approach

Our focus is on sentence-level work and instruction



Feedback
Describing Places: You did a nice job starting your writing with “In the summer breeze” …

Question
(Run-ons) Only one of the following is a correct sentence. Can you find it?

He conjured a breeze it smelled of summer.

The charm flared I stepped back.

The sorcerer appeared at once I gasped.

The lanterns glowed. We felt enchanted.

The air turned warm quickly it lifted my cloak.

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Targeted to ability


Highest Grade
(Logic & Inference) The trapped person speaks through a walkie-talkie. Which detail best implies they are nervous and impatient without directly saying it?

Their voice came in short bursts, words clipped, and the walkie-talkie crackled as if they kept pressing the button too hard.

Their voice sounded musical and steady, as if they were calmly reading a bedtime story.

They spoke slowly and formally, choosing each word like a careful teacher.

Their voice was deep and smooth, with a relaxed laugh at the end of every sentence.

They talked in long, drifting sentences, pausing often as if daydreaming.



Lowest Grade
(Capitals) Only one of the following uses capitals correctly. Can you find it?

the nurse packed the remedy.

The nurse packed the Remedy.

the Nurse packed the remedy.

The nurse packed the remedy.

The Nurse packed the remedy.

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text: Personalised

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
section: Adopting the tone of the writing
text:
Adopting the tone of the writing
Question
(Logic & Inference — People) The writer shows the child’s personality through an action in the extraordinary place. Which detail best implies the child is gentle and careful?

I cup a ladybug in my hands and set it on a tall blade of grass instead of flicking it away.

I sprint through the meadow, knocking over every dew-covered flower I can find.

I grab fistfuls of grass and yank until the roots snap out of the ground.

I hurl stones into the stream to see the biggest splash I can make.

I shout at the trees so loudly that my throat hurts.

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Challenges

Specificity vs hallucinations
Hallucinated Personalisation
Inappropriate content

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Specificity vs Hallucination Trade Off
too generic to be much help eg ‘use more imaginative word choices’ or ‘think about your capital letters.’
“What you can do better - always capitalise ocean and crocodile.” [Really?]
“What you can do better - always start a sentence with a capital letter.” [The pupil had]
“What you can do better - make sure you finish each sentence with a full stop.” [The pupil had]
The AI struggled providing feedback on work in which the pupils had done everything correctly.

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Hallucinated Personalisation







CautionFeedback labelled with the wrong name



It seems that in the cases where the feedback tries to say the student’s name first, it will often use the subject from the writing. For example, we had students called Martin Luther King, Jr., Rosa Parks, and John Deere (3 times). Is it possible to remove the option for it to personalize the report by naming the person in the first line?

title: Using AI to give feedback on writing tasks
text:
Inappropriate content

Kids score very highly on the violence scale in their writing
Should we personalise feedback differently for open-ended tasks?



Student’s story

“Bob, a frightened boy, runs into a dark forest after a fight with his brother, encounters a man called Ghost Face wearing a ghost mask who initially seems threatening but then offers friendship; however, Ghost Face traps Bob, attempts to kill him, and ultimately kills Bob near his home while his mother watches in horror.”


Question
(Run-ons) Only one of the following is a correct sentence. Can you find it?

He ran into the forest it was pitch-dark.

The branches snapped I froze.

The masked man appeared he whispered my name.

The porch light flickered. His mother screamed.

The fog thickened quickly it hid the path.

title: AI Enhanced CJ
text:
Issues: Hallucinated Transcriptions

The AI occasionally produces transcriptions that do not match the original handwritten script
The Fall of the House of Usher issue
Handwriting degrades rapidly under exam conditions
Requires mutliple models & post-hoc checking to identify and remove affected scripts

title: AI Enhanced CJ
text:
Issues: Weighting in Training Data

The AI’s training data may over-represent certain writing styles or authors
The Carol Ann Duffy issue: if you are writing about a poem by Carol …
This could introduce systematic bias in scores that is not present in human judging

title: AI Enhanced CJ
text:
Issues: Unexpected Responses

In some cases the AI produces responses outside the expected scoring range or format
The One Day issue: “1 Day.”
Very short responses when an essay is expected cause unexpected results
